corpus: syllabus-raw.txt — Element 9 verbatim parameters (lines ~1608-1666): 9.1 business-location supervision guideline components; 9.2 internal/reputational/business-activity/size/client-base risk-factor lists plus the 'Investment Dealers and its clients' impact framing; 9.3 retail distribution risk list including the 'advisory services and fully discretionary managed products' lead-in

idpc-rules.txt (IDPC Jan 2026) — Rule 1201 'business location' definition; 2201(1); 2216 (shared office premises); 2246(3) (highly-leveraged securities approval); 3219(1)/(2) (hold mail); 3602(2)/(6); 3905(2); 3925(4); 3926(2)/(4)/(6); 3927(3); 3945(3)/(4); 3946(1); 3947(2)/(3); 3970; 3971(2); 3972; 3973

gn-3900-20-001.txt — Account Supervision Guidance: resident-Supervisor necessity factors; central first-tier review conditions; first-tier daily (one business day) and monthly (21 days) timing; non-client account monthly inclusion; second-tier daily/monthly example criteria; split supervision between Supervisors; back-up during absences; pre-trade vs post-trade reviews; pattern reviews over longer periods; non-trade issues; employee and agent account activity; fee-based selection criteria
meta.sourcing_note: Sources per outcome — 9.1: syllabus-raw.txt 9.1 component list plus IDPC 2201(1), 3905(2), 3925(4), 3926(4)/(6) and GN-3900-20-001 (resident-Supervisor factors, central first-tier conditions, first-tier daily/monthly timing, split supervision, second-tier example criteria). 9.2: syllabus-raw.txt 9.2 parameter lists (exposure-inherent, lines-of-business/aggregate-portfolio, full business-activity list, category structure, impact-on-dealer-and-clients framing, inverse/NOT directions) plus IDPC 2216(6)/(11) and 3926(2) as control anchors. 9.3: syllabus-raw.txt 9.3 list (notably the previously untested 'advisory services and fully discretionary managed products' lead-in and 'Approved Person's failure ... high levels of integrity' item) plus IDPC Part G (3970 monthly discretionary review and non-delegation, 3971(2)(ii) fair allocation, 3972 committee, 3973 quarterly managed review), 3219(1)/(2) hold mail, 3927(3), 3945(3)/(4), 3946(1), 3947(2)/(3), 2246(3), 3602(2)/(6) and GN-3900-20-001 (pattern reviews, non-trade issues, employee/agent accounts, second-tier monthly commission criteria). Novelty gate: full tested-angle inventory built from SUP-E9-0001-0002 (seeds) and SUP-E9-0003-0052 (batch 001) before drafting; every batch-002 item tests an untested list item (e.g. exposure-inherent, lines-of-business, AP-integrity, hold-mail minimums, Part G discretionary/managed reviews, resident-Supervisor factors, review timing, fee-based criteria, highly-leveraged pre-approval), an untested pairing, or an inverse direction (NOT-membership, exemption vs duty, rule-content vs classification). Where a rule_ref repeats batch 001 (3219, 3926, 3927, 3947, 3602, GN-3900-20-001) the keyed fact/subsection or direction differs: 3219(1) hold-mail minimums and 3219(2) extension vs batch 001's 3219(3) returned mail; 3926(2) rule content vs batch 001's classification use; 3927(3) infraction-history measures vs 3927(2) evidence; 3947(2) exemption vs 3947(1) duty; 3602(2)/(6) P&P and retention vs 3602(3) pre-approval list; GN second-tier example criteria, timing and resident-factor lists vs batch 001's second-tier purpose/referral/outside-visit facts. Near-misses rewritten during automated scans: (1) a drafted 9.1 item keying the five-component list was cut for >0.5 token overlap with SUP-E9-0004 and replaced with the 'Scope' component-purpose item SUP-E9-0053; (2) a drafted 9.2 'which three items are internal factors' inverse was rewritten into the NOT-internal direction (SUP-E9-0067) after Jaccard ~0.55 vs SUP-E9-0015; (3) a drafted outside-Supervisor-visit item for SET-03 duplicated SUP-E9-0010/0048's keyed fact and was replaced with the resident-Supervisor necessity-factors item SUP-E9-0091; (4) SUP-E9-0068 was first drafted as a second NOT-membership item and scored Jaccard 0.52 against sibling SUP-E9-0067, so it was rewritten into a positive complete-category-grouping format keying the full business-activity trio (post-rewrite max within-batch Jaccard 0.48, max vs-existing 0.41 — both under the 0.5 rewrite line). Firm and person names all fresh — no reuse from seeds or batch 001 (new firms: Halverson, Quarrystone, Verecourt, Birchmount, Selwyn, Tamarind, Oakhurst, Drummond Bay, Petrel Cove, Whitfield, Anstruther, Rosemere, Calloway, Lindenfield, Harrowsmith, Mistaya, Bourassa & Finch, Trelawney, Silverthorn, Kabetogama). Honest exclusions: no item states a pass mark, calculator policy or GN-2600 numbering; 'number of registrants' is treated as a location-sizing consideration consistent with syllabus.json and batch 001, and no item turns on which parent bullet it sits under in the PDF extraction; GN dollar thresholds are framed as example criteria that would satisfy Rule 3900, matching the guidance's own wording.
meta.qa: Gate 1 blind solve 2026-06-05: 50/50 agreement (5 medium adjudicated via verified quotes). Gate 2: 50/50, 0 fabrications. Gate 3: 15 same-fact kills (14 cross-element vs E2/E3/E5/E6 reviewed items + 1 intra-batch); 2 SET-05 survivors held for E9-003 top-up. 33 merged. See qa-log.md wave 6.

questions.stem: Compliance at Ullswater Securities is drafting its business location supervision program and must first record which locations, activities and registrant populations the program will cover before any visit is scheduled. Within the syllabus 9.1 guideline components, this task corresponds to:

options.A: Audit report and follow up
options.B: Scope
options.C: Risk identification
options.D: Training

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.1 lists scope first among the business location supervision guidelines: defining which locations, activities and people the program covers is the scope decision that precedes everything else. Risk identification (option C) is the closest distractor, but it ranks the covered locations by risk after coverage has been defined — it does not decide what is covered. Option A documents results at the end of the cycle, and option D equips the audit staff rather than defining coverage.
questions.source_quote: "• Scope" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1613

questions.stem: Otterell Partners proposes to route all order tickets for one remote branch through an unrelated numbered company so that the branch's activity never appears in the dealer's supervision systems. Which foundational IDPC organizational requirement does this arrangement breach?

options.A: A dealer's business must be organized to enable adequate supervision of all of its activities and cannot be organized to avoid Corporation requirements
options.B: A dealer must pay an annual fee for each branch office member
options.C: A dealer must hold its annual meeting at head office
options.D: A dealer must obtain CIRO approval of every branch lease

questions.rationale: Subsection 2201(1) requires that a Dealer Member's business be organized to enable adequate supervision of all of its activities and provides that it cannot be organized to avoid Corporation requirements — routing one branch's activity outside the supervision system is organization-to-avoid. Option B is false: section 2268 says no annual or entrance fee is payable for branch office members, so it is the closest trap for candidates who recall Part D. Options C and D invent requirements that appear nowhere in Rule 2200.
questions.source_quote: "of its activities and cannot be organized to avoid Corporation requirements." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1282

questions.stem: When deciding how many Supervisors to appoint across its business locations, what does section 3905 of the IDPC Rules require an Investment Dealer to take into account?

options.A: The average age of its Registered Representatives
options.B: The number of CIRO Districts in which it operates
options.C: The scope and complexity of the Dealer Member's business
options.D: The supervisor-to-registrant ratios published annually by CIRO

questions.rationale: Subsection 3905(2) requires a dealer to appoint as many Supervisors as necessary to properly supervise its employees and Approved Persons taking into account the scope and complexity of the Dealer Member's business. Option D is the closest distractor because candidates expect a prescribed ratio, but the rule sets a principle, not a published ratio. Option B confuses geographic district count with the business-based test, and option A is irrelevant to supervisory capacity.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must appoint as many Supervisors as necessary to properly supervise its" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7923

questions.stem: The designated branch Supervisor at Jorvik Securities' Saskatoon location will be on parental leave for five months. Under section 3925 of the IDPC Rules, how must the dealer cover the supervisory function during the absence?

options.A: Suspend new account openings at the branch until the Supervisor returns
options.B: Appoint one or more alternate Supervisors to assume the responsibility of the designated Supervisor in their absence
options.C: Have the branch's senior sales assistant approve account activity informally
options.D: Ask CIRO staff to perform the daily reviews during the leave

questions.rationale: Subsection 3925(4) requires a dealer to appoint one or more alternate Supervisors as required, to the Supervisors designated in subsection 3925(2), to supervise the dealer's business and to assume the responsibility of the designated Supervisor in their absence; the guidance adds that back-up Supervisors should be aware of ongoing issues during prolonged absences. Option A is the closest distractor because it appears protective, but the rule's solution is continuity of supervision, not a business freeze. Option C assigns supervision to an unqualified, undesignated person, and option D shifts the dealer's own obligation onto the regulator.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must appoint one or more alternate Supervisors as required, to the Supervisors" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8091
questions.qa_note: KILLED Gate 3 wave 6 (2026-06-05): cross-element same-fact dup of reviewed SUP-E2-0029 (IDPC 3925(4) alternate-Supervisor appointment in absence; same keyed obligation, framing differs only).

questions.stem: A branch Supervisor at Skerries Capital is qualified to supervise equity and fixed-income activity but not the options business that three of her registrants conduct. According to CIRO's account supervision guidance, how may the dealer structure supervision at this location?

options.A: Divide the supervision between two or more Supervisors, provided there are appropriate communication mechanisms, the dealer maintains an overall view of each client's situation and activity, and the assignment of responsibilities is clear and complete
options.B: Let the unqualified Supervisor approve options activity so long as she completes the proficiency course within two years
options.C: Exempt the options accounts from supervision because no qualified resident Supervisor exists
options.D: Reassign the three registrants' clients to head office advisors

questions.rationale: The guidance states that if a Supervisor is unqualified to supervise trading activity in certain products, a dealer may divide the supervision between two or more Supervisors provided there are appropriate mechanisms for them to communicate with one another, the system ensures the dealer maintains an overall view of the client's situation and activity, and the assignment of responsibilities is clear and complete; a primary Supervisor advised by others is also permitted. Option B is the closest distractor — proficiency is required before supervising, not retroactively. Option C invents an exemption the guidance forecloses, and option D is a drastic remedy nothing requires when split supervision is available.
questions.source_quote: "there are appropriate mechanisms for them to communicate with one another," — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 136
questions.qa_note: KILLED Gate 3 wave 6 (2026-06-05): cross-element same-fact dup of reviewed SUP-E5-0059 (GN-3900-20-001/IDPC 3905(5) split supervision between Supervisors; same anchors, same keyed proposition, same scenario archetype).

questions.stem: Drummond Bay Securities wants its head office to conduct the first-tier review of trading at all twelve branches instead of relying on resident Supervisors. Under CIRO's account supervision guidance, this central arrangement is acceptable provided:

options.A: Each branch retains a shadow reviewer who repeats the head-office work
options.B: CIRO grants a written exemption from Rule 3900 first
options.C: There are resources available to conduct the review at that location and the dealer has adequate procedures for dealing with any issues identified
options.D: The dealer abandons second-tier reviews to avoid duplication

questions.rationale: The guidance says resident Supervisors will normally conduct the first-tier review at their respective business location, but dealers can conduct these reviews in a central location (head office or regional office) provided there are resources available to conduct the review at that location and the dealer has adequate procedures for dealing with any issues identified. Option B is the closest distractor — no exemption is needed because the guidance itself permits the central model. Option A imposes duplication the guidance does not require, and option D removes the distinct second-tier oversight function rather than satisfying any condition.
questions.source_quote: "there are resources available to conduct the review at that location, and" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 232
questions.qa_note: KILLED Gate 3 wave 6 (2026-06-05): cross-element same-fact dup of reviewed SUP-E5-0011 (GN-3900-20-001 s.2.1 centralized first-tier review conditions; same keyed conditions).

questions.stem: Absent unusual circumstances, within what time does CIRO's account supervision guidance expect a dealer to complete the first-tier daily review of a business location's trading?

options.A: Before the market opens on the day of the trading
options.B: Within 21 days of the trading activity
options.C: Within six months, aligned with the branch audit cycle
options.D: One business day after the trading activity occurs

questions.rationale: The guidance directs dealers to design their first-tier daily review of the previous day's trading to detect the section 3945 issues and to complete this review one business day after the trading activity occurs, unless precluded by unusual circumstances. Option B is the closest distractor — 21 days is the completion window the guidance sets for the monthly review, not the daily one. Option A describes a pre-trade model rather than the daily post-trade review timing, and option C confuses the trade-review cycle with location audit scheduling.
questions.source_quote: "issues in section 3945. They should complete this review one business day after the trading" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 239

questions.stem: In CIRO's account supervision guidance, the first-tier monthly review of a business location's retail activity should be completed within what period, unless precluded by unusual circumstances?

options.A: 21 days of the review period
options.B: One business day after month-end
options.C: 90 days of the review period
options.D: Seven years of the review period

questions.rationale: The guidance states that dealers should complete the first-tier monthly review within 21 days of the review period, unless precluded by unusual circumstances. Option B is the closest distractor because one business day is the guidance's completion expectation for daily reviews, not monthly ones. Option C has no basis in the guidance, and option D confuses the record-retention period in section 3803 with a review-completion deadline.
questions.source_quote: "Dealers should complete this review within 21 days of the review period, unless precluded" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 254

questions.stem: Beyond selected retail client accounts, which accounts does CIRO's guidance say a first-tier monthly review at a business location should include?

options.A: Only accounts that generated a client complaint during the month
options.B: All institutional accounts carried for other dealers
options.C: Only accounts opened during the month
options.D: All non-client accounts with any activity other than dividend and interest payments

questions.rationale: The guidance states that a first-tier monthly review should include all non-client accounts with any activity other than dividend and interest payments — employee and related accounts are a recognized misuse channel at any location. Option A is the closest distractor because complaint-flagged accounts certainly merit review, but the guidance's stated inclusion rule is about non-client accounts with activity, not complaints. Option C limits review to new accounts, which the guidance nowhere does, and option B points at institutional supervision, a different Part of Rule 3900.
questions.source_quote: "A ﬁrst-tier monthly review should include all non-client accounts with any activity other" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 251
questions.qa_note: KILLED Gate 3 wave 6 (2026-06-05): cross-element same-fact dup of reviewed SUP-E5-0025 (GN-3900-20-001 s.2.1.2 first-tier monthly review must include non-client accounts with activity other than dividend/interest; identical keyed text).

questions.stem: Petrel Cove Capital's head office is writing the criteria for its second-tier daily review of branch trading. Which criterion appears in CIRO's guidance as an example that would satisfy Rule 3900?

options.A: All trades executed before 10:00 a.m. Eastern time
options.B: Trades in accounts whose holders were born in even-numbered years
options.C: All client accounts not reviewed by a branch manager
options.D: Only trades the client has complained about in writing

questions.rationale: Among the example second-tier daily review criteria the guidance says would satisfy Rule 3900 is all client accounts not reviewed by a branch manager — second-tier review closes the gap where first-tier coverage is absent, alongside criteria such as producing branch managers' client accounts, trade cancellations and outstanding margin calls. Option D is the closest distractor because complaints do trigger separate handling under 3946(1), but a written-complaint filter is not the listed second-tier daily criterion. Options A and B are arbitrary filters unconnected to risk and appear nowhere in the guidance.
questions.source_quote: "all client accounts not reviewed by a branch manager," — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 284

questions.stem: Under subsection 3926(4) of the IDPC Rules, what must an Investment Dealer provide in writing to all supervisory staff across its business locations?

options.A: A guarantee of indemnity against client lawsuits
options.B: The procedures to be followed in reviewing account activity and confirmation of the dealer's expectations of supervisory staff with respect to their supervisory roles and responsibilities
options.C: Quarterly profit-sharing statements for their branch
options.D: A copy of every other branch's audit findings

questions.rationale: Subsection 3926(4) requires the dealer to provide all supervisory staff with written procedures to be followed in reviewing account activity and written confirmation of the Dealer Member's expectations of supervisory staff, with respect to their supervisory roles and responsibilities — Supervisors at every location must know both how to review and what is expected of them. Option D is the closest distractor because audit findings do circulate to those responsible for follow-up, but the rule's written deliverables are procedures and expectations, not other branches' findings. Options A and C concern compensation and liability, outside section 3926 entirely.
questions.source_quote: "procedures to be followed in reviewing account activity, and" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8108
questions.qa_note: KILLED Gate 3 wave 6 (2026-06-05): cross-element same-fact dup of reviewed SUP-E2-0084 and SUP-E5-0061 (IDPC 3926(4) written procedures + written confirmation of expectations; same keyed proposition).

questions.stem: Penhale Securities last refreshed its branch supervision manual nine years ago, before several regulatory reforms. Which IDPC requirement does this stale state most directly offend?

options.A: The dealer must periodically review the policies and procedures used at its head office and its branch offices to provide reasonable assurance they continue to be effective and reflect current legislative and regulatory requirements
options.B: The dealer must reprint its manual whenever CIRO changes its logo
options.C: The dealer must file its manual with CIRO every December
options.D: Branch policies expire automatically after five years

questions.rationale: Subsection 3926(6) requires a dealer to periodically review the policies and procedures used at its head office and its branch offices to provide reasonable assurance the policies and procedures continue to be effective and reflect current legislative and regulatory requirements, as well as industry practices — a nine-year-old manual that predates reforms fails that periodic-review obligation. Option D is the closest distractor because it sounds like a freshness rule, but no automatic expiry exists; the duty is periodic review, not sunset. Options B and C invent filing and reprinting obligations found nowhere in the rule.
questions.source_quote: "its branch offices to provide reasonable assurance the policies and procedures continue to be" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8114
questions.qa_note: KILLED Gate 3 wave 6 (2026-06-05): cross-element same-fact dup of reviewed SUP-E5-0062 (IDPC 3926(6) periodic review of head-office/branch P&Ps; same keyed obligation, same stale-policies scenario archetype).

questions.stem: Anstruther Capital's Yellowknife branch is the only one in the firm permitted to distribute a structured note with embedded leverage; every other branch sells conventional securities. In the syllabus 9.2 framework, the elevated risk this single product creates for that branch is classified as:

options.A: Key person reliance, an internal risk factor
options.B: Number of complaints, a reputational risk factor
options.C: Exposure inherent to providing a particular product or service at a business location — a business activity risk
options.D: Risk assessment and control practices, a size risk consideration

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.2 lists, within the business location's business activity risk, the exposure inherent to providing a particular product or service at a business location — a leveraged structured note sold only at one branch concentrates that inherent product exposure there. Option D is the closest distractor because controls must respond to the exposure, but risk assessment and control practices is a size-risk consideration describing the location's control maturity, not the product exposure itself. Option A concerns dependence on an individual and option B concerns complaint volume, neither of which the facts raise.
questions.source_quote: "- Exposure inherent to providing a particular product or service at a" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1631

questions.stem: Rosemere Financial's Victoria branch runs retail advisory, options, new-issue distribution and a fee-based managed program side by side, and head office wants to assess how those businesses combine. Which syllabus 9.2 business activity consideration speaks directly to this combined assessment?

options.A: Any internal disciplinary action
options.B: Adequacy of resources
options.C: The business location's lines of business and the aggregate risk present in its overall portfolio
options.D: Community-specific marketing strategy and sales practices

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.2 lists, under business activity risk, the business location's lines of business and the aggregate risk present in its overall portfolio — exactly the combined, portfolio-level view of multiple businesses run at one location. Option B is the closest distractor because more lines of business strain resources, but adequacy of resources is an internal factor about capacity, not about how the businesses aggregate. Options A and D are reputational factors about conduct signals, not about the mix of businesses.
questions.source_quote: "- Business location’s lines of business and the aggregate risk present" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1633

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the internal risk factors listed for business locations in syllabus 9.2?

options.A: Adequacy of resources
options.B: Key person reliance
options.C: Principal-agent
options.D: Number of complaints

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.2's internal risk factors are adequacy of resources, key person reliance and principal-agent. Number of complaints is a real 9.2 item, but it sits in the reputational risk list, making option D the item that does not belong. Options A, B and C each name a listed internal factor, so none can be the exception.
questions.source_quote: "- Adequacy of resources" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1622

questions.stem: A risk analyst at Petrel Cove Capital must populate the "business location's business activity risk" section of the firm's location-rating template using only items that syllabus 9.2 places in that category. Which grouping should she use?

options.A: Key person reliance; principal-agent; adequacy of resources
options.B: Exposure inherent to providing a particular product or service at the location; the location's lines of business and the aggregate risk present in its overall portfolio; number of trading errors
options.C: Community-specific marketing strategy and sales practices; any internal disciplinary action; number of complaints
options.D: Risk-weighting; risk assessment and control practices

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.2's business activity risk list comprises the exposure inherent to providing a particular product or service at a business location, the business location's lines of business and the aggregate risk present in its overall portfolio, and the number of trading errors. Option D is the closest distractor because controls relate to activity, but risk-weighting and risk assessment and control practices form the business location size risk pair. Option A reproduces the internal risk factors and option C reproduces reputational indicators — real 9.2 items, wrong category.
questions.source_quote: "• Business location’s business activity risk, including:" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1630
questions.qa_note: KILLED Gate 3 wave 6 (2026-06-05): intra-batch same-fact dup of SUP-E9-0095 (syllabus 9.2 business-activity-risk membership: exposure-inherent + lines-of-business; identical keyed pair). Set item 0095 retained as better use of the fact.

questions.stem: According to the opening text of syllabus 9.2, a Supervisor considers additional risk factors of using different business locations because of their potential impact on whom?

options.A: CIRO's enforcement department and hearing panels
options.B: The Investment Dealers and its clients
options.C: The location's landlord and insurers
options.D: Competing dealers in the same Region

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.2 directs the Supervisor to understand additional risk factors and the potential impact on the Investment Dealers and its clients of using different business locations — the framework exists to protect both the firm and the people it serves. Option A is the closest distractor because location failures can end in enforcement, but enforcement is a consequence, not the stated impact population. Options C and D name commercial third parties the syllabus does not mention.
questions.source_quote: "Investment Dealers and its clients of using different business locations." — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1619

questions.stem: Vimont Securities' Moose Jaw branch blankets local radio with promotions promising "farm-smart investing for our community" and stages pressure-heavy seminars timed to harvest payouts. Under syllabus 9.2, which reputational risk item do these local tactics engage?

options.A: Number of trading errors
options.B: Number of registrants
options.C: Principal-agent
options.D: Community-specific marketing strategy and sales practices

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.2 lists community-specific marketing strategy and sales practices as a reputational risk of a business location — locally targeted promotion and pressure-driven sales tactics are precisely the conduct that can damage the dealer's standing in that community. Option A is the closest categorical trap because aggressive selling can eventually produce errors, but trading errors is a business activity metric and nothing in the facts describes execution mistakes. Option B is a sizing consideration and option C is an internal structural factor, neither describing marketing conduct.
questions.source_quote: "- Community-specific marketing strategy and sales practices" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1626
questions.qa_note: KILLED Gate 3 wave 6 (2026-06-05): same-fact dup of reviewed SUP-E9-0018 (syllabus 9.2 'community-specific marketing strategy and sales practices' = reputational risk factor; identical keyed text, same membership mapping).

questions.stem: Head office at Lindenfield Capital is rolling out cross-training so that at least two people at each branch can run supervision, production support and back-office workflows. Which syllabus 9.2 internal risk factor is this program designed to mitigate?

options.A: Number of outside activities
options.B: Client base risk
options.C: Key person reliance
options.D: Exposure inherent to a particular product

questions.rationale: Key person reliance is the syllabus 9.2 internal risk factor describing a location's dependence on one individual; cross-training a second person in each critical workflow directly reduces that dependence. Option B is the closest distractor in form because it is also a standalone risk concept, but client base risk concerns clients using the location for inappropriate actions, which staffing redundancy does not address. Option A is a reputational indicator about a registrant's external involvements, and option D is a product-exposure measure — neither is touched by cross-training.
questions.source_quote: "- Key person reliance" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1623

questions.stem: Which pairing correctly matches a syllabus 9.2 risk item to its category?

options.A: Number of trading errors — reputational risk
options.B: Risk-weighting — business location size risk
options.C: Adequacy of resources — client base risk
options.D: Number of complaints — internal risk factor

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.2 lists risk-weighting under business location size risk, together with risk assessment and control practices. Option A misfiles trading errors, which belong to business activity risk — the closest trap because errors do embarrass a branch, but the syllabus categorizes the count as an activity metric. Option C misfiles adequacy of resources, an internal factor, and option D misfiles number of complaints, a reputational factor.
questions.source_quote: "Business location size risk, including: - Risk-weighting" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 1663-1664

questions.stem: To manage the client base risk that syllabus 9.2 assigns to every business location, subsection 3926(2) of the IDPC Rules requires a dealer's policies and procedures to specifically address its obligations to do which of the following?

options.A: Identify clients that present a high risk to the dealer, identify clients that present a high risk of conducting improper activities in the securities markets, and comply with all anti-money laundering and terrorist financing requirements under applicable laws
options.B: Refuse accounts for any client residing more than 100 kilometres from a branch
options.C: Report every cash deposit at a branch to the local police service
options.D: Obtain CIRO pre-approval before accepting politically exposed clients

questions.rationale: Subsection 3926(2) requires policies and procedures that specifically address the dealer's obligations to identify clients that present a high risk to the Dealer Member, identify clients that present a high risk of conducting improper activities in the securities markets, and comply with all anti-money laundering and terrorist financing requirements under applicable laws — the control framework for the 9.2 risk that clients may use a location for inappropriate actions. Option C is the closest distractor because suspicious activity does get reported, but the statutory channel is AML reporting under applicable laws, not blanket police reports of every deposit. Option B invents a residency bar and option D invents a CIRO pre-approval step, neither of which exists.
questions.source_quote: "identify clients that present a high risk to the Dealer Member," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8099

questions.stem: Mardale Capital's Brandon location shares premises with a mortgage brokerage. To control the location risk to client information that this arrangement creates, what does section 2216 of the IDPC Rules require for the physical layout and operation of the shared premises?

options.A: A separate street entrance for each entity's clients
options.B: That all client meetings occur off-site at neutral venues
options.C: That the premises be laid out and operated in a manner that ensures the control and confidentiality of client information and client records, with client records and account process areas effectively controlled and physically secure
options.D: That the mortgage brokerage staff sign CIRO membership applications

questions.rationale: Subsection 2216(6) requires a dealer's shared office premises to be laid out and operated in a manner that ensures the control and confidentiality of client information and client records by ensuring that client records and account process areas are effectively controlled and physically secure. Option A is the closest distractor because separation matters, but the rule requires prominent display of legal names and differentiating signage, not separate entrances. Option B has no basis in the rule, and option D confuses sharing premises with becoming a Dealer Member.
questions.source_quote: "client records and account process areas are effectively controlled and physically secure." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1398

questions.stem: At a shared business location, how must an Investment Dealer keep its electronic client records relative to the co-located financial services entity under subsection 2216(11)?

options.A: On a shared drive with read-only access for the other entity
options.B: With separate passwords or another similar control to ensure the financial services entity has no access to the dealer's electronic client records
options.C: Printed and stored off-site, with electronic copies deleted weekly
options.D: Merged with the other entity's records if both firms have common ownership

questions.rationale: Subsection 2216(11) requires client records to be kept separate from those of the other financial services entity: the entity must not have access to the client's hard copy records, and electronic records must have separate passwords or another similar control to ensure the financial services entity has no access to the dealer's electronic client records. Option A is the closest distractor — even read-only access is access, which the rule forbids. Option C invents a deletion regime that would breach retention requirements, and option D is wrong because the separation duty applies whether or not the entities are related or affiliated.
questions.source_quote: "electronic records must have separate passwords or another similar control to ensure the" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1417

questions.stem: Kelsale Securities scores each location's size risk before setting oversight intensity. Two branches sell identical products, but Branch P has 26 registrants and a layered control function while Branch Q has 4 registrants and informal controls. How should the syllabus 9.2 size-risk considerations shape the comparison?

options.A: Size risk is assessed through risk-weighting and the location's risk assessment and control practices, so Branch P's larger registrant base raises its weighting while its stronger control practices partially offset, and Branch Q's weak practices count against its small size
options.B: Only revenue determines size risk, so the comparison cannot be made
options.C: Branch Q automatically scores higher risk because small branches always rank above large ones
options.D: Size risk ignores controls entirely; the registrant count alone decides the score

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.2's business location size risk comprises risk-weighting and risk assessment and control practices, and the number of registrants is a listed sizing consideration — so a proper comparison weights Branch P's scale upward while crediting its control maturity, and penalizes Branch Q's informal practices despite its size. Option D is the closest distractor because registrant count matters, but it wrongly excludes the listed control-practices consideration. Option C inverts the weighting logic into an absolute rule the framework does not state, and option B substitutes a revenue-only test that appears nowhere in the list.
questions.source_quote: "- Risk assessment and control practices" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1665

questions.stem: According to its opening text, syllabus 9.3 requires a Supervisor to apply supervisory requirements to retail distribution risks including which two service categories?

options.A: Proprietary trading and institutional sales
options.B: Carrying-broker services and clearing
options.C: Investment banking and research distribution
options.D: Advisory services and fully discretionary managed products

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.3 directs the Supervisor to apply supervisory requirements to retail distribution risks, including advisory services and fully discretionary managed products, to specific situations relevant to a Supervisor. Option A is the closest distractor because trading supervision exists elsewhere in the rules, but 9.3's stated coverage is retail distribution, not proprietary or institutional activity. Options B and C name back-office and capital-markets functions outside the retail distribution frame.
questions.source_quote: "advisory services and fully discretionary managed products to specific" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1641

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT among the retail distribution risks listed in syllabus 9.3?

options.A: Inadequate control over books and records, with particular attention to poor control of client address changes
options.B: New or complex products that are not understood by retail investors
options.C: Key person reliance
options.D: Improper reassignment of terminated registrants' accounts

questions.rationale: Key person reliance is a real Element 9 concept, but it belongs to the syllabus 9.2 internal risk factors of business locations, not the 9.3 retail distribution risk list. Options A, B and D each quote risks that appear on the 9.3 list, so none can be the exception. The trap is mis-categorization between the two adjacent parameter lists rather than invented text.
questions.source_quote: "New or complex products that are not understood by retail investors" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1655

questions.stem: A retired teacher tells the Supervisor at Bourassa & Finch Securities that her RR forged her initials on a switch form, misrepresented a fund's redemption fee, and ignored her written instruction to stop trading. Applying syllabus 9.3, this pattern is best characterized as which listed retail distribution risk?

options.A: Business location size risk
options.B: Inadequate follow-up procedures on compliance audit findings
options.C: Failure to meet requirements for managing the margin activities of clients
options.D: An Approved Person's failure to meet regulatory, contractual and legal obligations and to apply high levels of integrity

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.3 lists the Approved Person's failure to meet regulatory, contractual and legal obligations and to apply high levels of integrity as a retail distribution risk — forging initials, misrepresenting fees and defying written client instructions is a direct integrity and obligations failure by the registrant. Option B is the closest structural distractor but requires a prior audit finding left untracked, which the facts do not mention. Option C requires margin activity, absent here, and option A imports a 9.2 location-size concept into a 9.3 conduct question.
questions.source_quote: "obligations and to apply high levels of integrity" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1657

questions.stem: An RR at Trelawney Securities returns from a regulatory suspension for discretionary trading without authorization. Under subsection 3927(3) of the IDPC Rules, what must the dealer have in place for this registrant?

options.A: A permanent ban on the RR holding any retail accounts
options.B: Established procedures for the implementation of additional supervisory measures applicable to Approved Persons with a history of regulatory infractions or questionable conduct
options.C: An exemption from daily trade reviews, since the suspension already punished the conduct
options.D: A requirement that the RR work only from head office

questions.rationale: Subsection 3927(3) requires a dealer to establish and follow procedures for the implementation of additional supervisory measures applicable to Approved Persons with a history of regulatory infractions or questionable conduct — heightened, not relaxed, oversight follows a disciplinary history. Option D is the closest distractor because relocation can be one such measure, but the rule mandates procedures for additional measures, not a fixed head-office posting. Option A overstates the rule into a ban it does not impose, and option C inverts it entirely.
questions.source_quote: "supervisory measures applicable to Approved Persons with a history of regulatory infractions or" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8132

questions.stem: A snowbird client of Silverthorn Securities asks her branch to stop mailing statements while she winters abroad. Under subsection 3219(1) of the IDPC Rules, the dealer's hold-mail procedures for retail clients must, at a minimum, include which provision?

options.A: A requirement that the dealer obtain written authorization from the client to hold mail
options.B: A requirement that the RR personally store the held mail at his residence
options.C: A rule permitting hold-mail orders to run indefinitely if the client is travelling
options.D: A requirement that held mail be destroyed after 90 days

questions.rationale: Subsection 3219(1) sets the minimums for retail hold-mail procedures: a requirement that the dealer obtain written authorization from the client to hold mail, a limit so a hold-mail order remains in force no longer than six months in any 12-month period, and a rule requiring control and regular review of hold-mail accounts by a Supervisor. Option C is the closest distractor because extensions are possible under 3219(2), but only with policies and procedures and Supervisor pre-approval — never indefinitely by default. Option B routes client mail to the very person hold-mail controls guard against, and option D invents a destruction rule that would eliminate records.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member’s hold‐mail account procedures for retail clients must, at a minimum, include" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4879

questions.stem: Without an approved extension, what is the maximum time a retail "hold mail" order may remain in force under clause 3219(1)(ii) of the IDPC Rules?

options.A: No fixed limit, provided the account is dormant
options.B: No longer than 30 days, in any calendar year
options.C: No longer than 12 months, renewable verbally
options.D: No longer than six months, in any 12 month period

questions.rationale: Clause 3219(1)(ii) requires a provision that limits the length of time a hold-mail order may remain in force for no longer than six months, in any 12 month period. Option C is the closest distractor because a longer period is possible — but only under 3219(2), which requires the dealer's policies and procedures to permit it, policies specifically addressing close supervision of such accounts, and pre-approval by the appropriate Supervisor, none of which is verbal renewal. Option B understates the limit and option A removes it entirely, defeating the anti-concealment purpose of the control.
questions.source_quote: "for no longer than six months, in any 12 month period, and" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4884

questions.stem: Kabetogama Capital wants to let certain long-absent clients keep hold-mail arrangements beyond the standard limit. Under subsection 3219(2) of the IDPC Rules, a longer period may be used only if:

options.A: The client pays a monthly hold-mail fee
options.B: The branch manager emails CIRO a list of the affected accounts
options.C: It is permitted by the dealer's policies and procedures, the dealer has policies and procedures that specifically address the close supervision of such accounts, and the appropriate Supervisor pre-approves the extended period
options.D: The RR servicing the account countersigns the original authorization

questions.rationale: Subsection 3219(2) permits exceeding the clause 3219(1)(ii) limit only where it is permitted by the dealer's policies and procedures, the dealer has policies and procedures that specifically address the close supervision of such accounts, and the appropriate Supervisor pre-approves the extended period. Option D is the closest distractor because a signature sounds procedural, but the RR is the conflicted party — approval must come from the appropriate Supervisor, not the servicing registrant. Option A invents a fee condition and option B invents a CIRO notification, neither of which the rule contains.
questions.source_quote: "the appropriate Supervisor pre‐approves the extended period." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4894
questions.qa_note: KILLED Gate 3 wave 6 (2026-06-05): cross-element same-fact dup of reviewed SUP-E5-0029 (IDPC 3219(2) conditions for longer hold-mail period; same keyed conditions).

questions.stem: Ullswater Securities plans to begin offering retail clients a newly engineered triple-leveraged volatility note. Under subsection 2246(3) of the IDPC Rules, what must the dealer do before offering it?

options.A: Nothing beyond internal product due diligence, since notification rules cover only name changes
options.B: Notify CIRO in writing and receive written approval from the Corporation before offering retail clients any highly-leveraged securities or derivatives
options.C: Post the product's term sheet on its public website for 30 days
options.D: Obtain a personal indemnity from each selling RR

questions.rationale: Subsection 2246(3) requires a dealer to notify in writing and receive written approval from the Corporation before offering retail clients any highly-leveraged securities or derivatives, or previously approved highly-leveraged products based on a new underlying interest — a regulatory gate that sits ahead of the 9.3 risk of new or complex products that are not understood by retail investors. Option A is the closest distractor because internal due diligence is also required, but for this product class the rule adds prior written CIRO approval. Options C and D invent disclosure and indemnity steps found nowhere in the rule.
questions.source_quote: "(i)      offering retail clients any highly-leveraged securities or derivatives, or" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1598

questions.stem: Otterell Partners selects branch retail accounts for monthly review purely by ranking gross commissions, even though a third of its book is fee-based. Under subsection 3945(3) of the IDPC Rules, what must its policies and procedures do for accounts where no commission is charged for trades?

options.A: Exclude fee-based accounts from activity review because no commission incentive exists
options.B: Review fee-based accounts only when the client requests it
options.C: Apply the identical commission-ranking screen, treating fees as commissions
options.D: Address account activity review Corporation requirements and use criteria other than commission levels

questions.rationale: Subsection 3945(3) requires policies and procedures that specifically address supervising retail client accounts where a commission is not charged for trades, such as fee based accounts, and those policies must address account activity review Corporation requirements and use criteria other than commission levels; the guidance likewise says a dealer cannot select accounts for review solely based on commission levels. Option C is the closest distractor because it sounds like an adaptation, but a commission-ranking screen by definition fails the criteria-other-than-commission requirement. Option A wrongly equates absent commissions with absent risk, and option B makes supervision client-initiated, which no rule permits.
questions.source_quote: "use criteria other than commission levels." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8179
questions.qa_note: KILLED Gate 3 wave 6 (2026-06-05): cross-element same-fact dup of reviewed SUP-E3-0042 (IDPC 3945(3) fee-based/non-commission review criteria; same keyed proposition).

questions.stem: Under subsection 3945(4) of the IDPC Rules, which retail client accounts must an Investment Dealer specifically designate for supervision purposes?

options.A: Cash accounts, DRIP accounts, TFSA accounts, USD accounts and corporate accounts
options.B: Non-client accounts, discretionary accounts, managed accounts, registered accounts and restricted accounts
options.C: Accounts opened online, accounts over $1 million, and accounts of seniors
options.D: Only accounts that have generated a complaint in the past 12 months

questions.rationale: Subsection 3945(4) requires the dealer to specifically designate non-client accounts, discretionary accounts, managed accounts, registered accounts and restricted accounts for supervision purposes — categories carrying elevated or distinct review obligations. Option A is the closest distractor because it mixes plausible account labels, but none of them is on the rule's designation list (registered accounts is the rule's term, not TFSA specifically as a designated class alongside cash and currency types). Option C describes risk-based selection criteria a dealer might add, not the prescribed designations, and option D substitutes a complaint trigger for the rule's standing designations.
questions.source_quote: "The Dealer Member must specifically designate the following retail client accounts for supervision" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8180
questions.qa_note: KILLED Gate 3 wave 6 (2026-06-05): cross-element same-fact dup of reviewed SUP-E5-0021 (IDPC 3945(4) designation list; identical keyed text, same direction).

questions.stem: Every individual trade in a retail account at Skerries Capital's Gatineau branch passes the daily suitability screen, yet over eight months the RR has rotated the entire portfolio six times. According to CIRO's account supervision guidance, why should the Supervisor's review design catch this?

options.A: Patterns of activity may not be apparent by reviewing single trades; reviewing trades over a longer period may detect issues with the overall account activity even if each trade is suitable for the client
options.B: Daily reviews are optional whenever monthly reviews exist
options.C: Portfolio turnover is exclusively a marketplace surveillance matter for CIRO
options.D: Suitability obligations end once a trade settles

questions.rationale: The guidance warns that patterns of activity may not be apparent by reviewing single trades, and that if a dealer reviews trades over a longer period, they may detect issues with the overall account activity even if each trade is suitable for the client — serial rotation is the classic pattern that single-trade screens miss, and excessive trading is a listed 3945(2) detection item. Option C is the closest distractor because CIRO does surveil markets, but account-level churning detection is the dealer's supervisory duty. Option B inverts the relationship between daily and monthly reviews, and option D misstates suitability, which attaches to the account relationship, not a settlement date.
questions.source_quote: "reviews trades over a longer period, they may detect issues with the overall account" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 191
questions.qa_note: KILLED Gate 3 wave 6 (2026-06-05): cross-element same-fact dup of reviewed SUP-E3-0088 (GN-3900-20-001 pattern-review principle: patterns not apparent in single trades; same keyed proposition).

questions.stem: Designing a branch review program limited strictly to executed trades, the Supervisor at Jorvik Securities asks what else CIRO's guidance expects reviews to cover. According to the guidance, dealers should also review non-trade issues such as:

options.A: Branch utility costs, parking allocations and equipment leases
options.B: Late payment, margin problems, trade cancellations or suspicious transfers and flows of funds or securities
options.C: Employee vacation schedules and overtime claims
options.D: The marketing budget of each Registered Representative

questions.rationale: The guidance states that dealers should review non-trade issues such as late payment, margin problems, trade cancellations or suspicious transfers and flows of funds or securities — the non-trade signals through which margin-management failures and improper money movement surface at a location. Option D is the closest distractor because marketing does require supervisory review, but that is the separate advertising-review regime under Rule 3600, not the non-trade account-activity items the guidance lists here. Options A and C are administrative matters with no account-supervision content.
questions.source_quote: "Dealers should review non-trade issues such as late payment, margin problems, trade" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 194

questions.stem: Penhale Securities' branch review program samples only customer accounts, never the accounts of its RRs or of the agents operating one of its locations. What does CIRO's account supervision guidance say about this design?

options.A: It is acceptable, because staff accounts are private
options.B: It is acceptable if each RR signs a quarterly self-review attestation
options.C: It is deficient — dealers should include employee and agent account activity in their review
options.D: It is deficient only for branches with more than ten registrants

questions.rationale: The guidance states that dealers should include employee and agent account activity in their review — staff and agent accounts are a recognized channel for improper trading, and section 3945(2) separately requires detection of improper or excessive employee trading. Option B is the closest distractor because attestations exist in compliance programs, but self-attestation by the account holder is not the independent review the guidance expects. Option A asserts a privacy carve-out that does not exist for supervisory review, and option D invents a headcount threshold found nowhere in the guidance.
questions.source_quote: "Dealers should include employee and agent account activity in their review." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 204

questions.stem: Head office at Rosemere Financial is drafting example criteria for its second-tier monthly review of branch retail activity. Which criterion does CIRO's guidance give as one that would satisfy Rule 3900?

options.A: Accounts of clients charged more than $50 in commission during the month
options.B: Accounts holding any exchange-traded fund
options.C: Accounts whose statements were mailed late
options.D: Accounts of clients charged more than $3,000 in commission during the month

questions.rationale: The guidance's example second-tier monthly review criteria include accounts of clients charged more than $3,000 in commission during the month, and accounts of all clients and non-clients charged more than $1,500 in commissions during the month not subject to a first-tier level review, including producing first level Supervisors' client accounts. Option A is the closest distractor because it copies the structure with an implausibly low threshold that appears nowhere in the guidance. Option B targets a product class rather than activity intensity, and option C describes an operations problem, not a stated review-selection criterion.
questions.source_quote: "accounts of clients charged more than $3,000 in commission during the month, and" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 301

questions.scenario: Wystan Securities, an Investment Dealer based in Halifax, is absorbing a five-office retail network in northern Ontario after an acquisition. None of the five offices currently has a resident Supervisor. Chief Compliance Officer Ines Beaudry is redesigning location supervision before the conversion date. Her working paper proposes: (a) a decision matrix for whether each office needs a resident Supervisor; (b) first-tier trade reviews performed centrally at the Halifax head office rather than at each office; (c) a calendar committing first-tier daily reviews to completion one business day after trading and first-tier monthly reviews within 21 days of the review period, absent unusual circumstances; and (d) second-tier daily criteria that include producing branch managers' client accounts and all client accounts not reviewed by a branch manager. One office, in Timmins, has two recently licensed RRs, no experienced staff, a speculative-resource client base, and the nearest Supervisor four hours away.
questions.stem: For Beaudry's decision matrix in item (a), which factors does CIRO's account supervision guidance say a dealer can use to determine the necessity of a resident Supervisor?

options.A: The office's square footage, lease term and signage budget
options.B: The number of Registered Individuals in the location, their experience, the business conducted in the location, Supervisor availability in nearby locations, and other systems and controls mitigating remote supervision risk
options.C: Whether the office's clients voted to request one
options.D: Only whether the office is profitable enough to fund the salary

questions.rationale: The guidance lets dealers determine the necessity of a resident Supervisor based on the number of Registered Individuals in the location, Registered Individuals' experience in the location, the business conducted in the location, Supervisor availability in nearby locations, and other systems and controls mitigating remote supervision risk. Option D is the closest distractor because cost pressures exist in practice, but profitability is not a listed factor and cannot substitute for the supervision analysis. Options A and C invent facility and client-vote considerations that appear nowhere in the guidance.
questions.source_quote: "other systems and controls mitigating remote supervision risk." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 167
questions.qa_note: KILLED Gate 3 wave 6 (2026-06-05): cross-element same-fact dup of reviewed SUP-E3-0057 (GN-3900-20-001 s.1.1.3 resident-Supervisor necessity factors; same keyed factor list). E9-SET-03 reduced to 3 items.

questions.scenario: Wystan Securities, an Investment Dealer based in Halifax, is absorbing a five-office retail network in northern Ontario after an acquisition. None of the five offices currently has a resident Supervisor. Chief Compliance Officer Ines Beaudry is redesigning location supervision before the conversion date. Her working paper proposes: (a) a decision matrix for whether each office needs a resident Supervisor; (b) first-tier trade reviews performed centrally at the Halifax head office rather than at each office; (c) a calendar committing first-tier daily reviews to completion one business day after trading and first-tier monthly reviews within 21 days of the review period, absent unusual circumstances; and (d) second-tier daily criteria that include producing branch managers' client accounts and all client accounts not reviewed by a branch manager. One office, in Timmins, has two recently licensed RRs, no experienced staff, a speculative-resource client base, and the nearest Supervisor four hours away.
questions.stem: Applying the guidance's resident-Supervisor factors to the Timmins office described in the scenario, which conclusion is best supported?

options.A: The factors weigh toward stronger on-site supervision: the registrants are inexperienced, the business is speculative, and no Supervisor is available nearby, so remote arrangements alone would need substantial mitigating systems and controls
options.B: No resident Supervisor can be needed because the office has only two registrants
options.C: The four-hour distance is irrelevant since reviews are electronic
options.D: The speculative client base reduces supervision needs because such clients accept risk

questions.rationale: The guidance's factors — number of Registered Individuals, their experience in the location, the business conducted, Supervisor availability in nearby locations, and other systems and controls mitigating remote supervision risk — cut toward closer supervision here: inexperience, speculative business and no nearby Supervisor all aggravate, and only strong mitigating systems could justify a remote model. Option B is the closest distractor because low headcount alone can support remote supervision, but the factors are weighed together, not singly. Option C ignores the listed Supervisor-availability factor, and option D confuses client risk tolerance with the dealer's supervision obligation, which the speculative business heightens rather than reduces.
questions.source_quote: "the number of Registered Individuals in the location," — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 163

questions.scenario: Wystan Securities, an Investment Dealer based in Halifax, is absorbing a five-office retail network in northern Ontario after an acquisition. None of the five offices currently has a resident Supervisor. Chief Compliance Officer Ines Beaudry is redesigning location supervision before the conversion date. Her working paper proposes: (a) a decision matrix for whether each office needs a resident Supervisor; (b) first-tier trade reviews performed centrally at the Halifax head office rather than at each office; (c) a calendar committing first-tier daily reviews to completion one business day after trading and first-tier monthly reviews within 21 days of the review period, absent unusual circumstances; and (d) second-tier daily criteria that include producing branch managers' client accounts and all client accounts not reviewed by a branch manager. One office, in Timmins, has two recently licensed RRs, no experienced staff, a speculative-resource client base, and the nearest Supervisor four hours away.
questions.stem: A director challenges item (b), insisting that first-tier reviews must always be performed by a resident Supervisor at each office. Under CIRO's account supervision guidance, is the director correct?

options.A: Yes — first-tier review may never leave the business location
options.B: Yes — central review is permitted only for institutional accounts
options.C: No — although resident Supervisors will normally conduct the first-tier review at their business location, dealers can conduct these reviews in a central location such as head office or a regional office, subject to the guidance's resource and procedure conditions
options.D: No — because first-tier reviews are entirely optional for acquired offices in their first year

questions.rationale: The guidance states that resident Supervisors will normally conduct the first-tier review at their respective business location, but dealers can conduct these reviews in a central location (head office or regional office) provided there are resources available to conduct the review at that location and the dealer has adequate procedures for dealing with any issues identified. Option A converts the word normally into a prohibition the guidance does not impose. Option B invents an institutional-only carve-out, and option D invents a first-year holiday from supervision that exists nowhere — acquired locations are supervised from day one.
questions.source_quote: "Resident Supervisors will normally conduct the ﬁrst-tier review at their respective business" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 228

questions.scenario: Wystan Securities, an Investment Dealer based in Halifax, is absorbing a five-office retail network in northern Ontario after an acquisition. None of the five offices currently has a resident Supervisor. Chief Compliance Officer Ines Beaudry is redesigning location supervision before the conversion date. Her working paper proposes: (a) a decision matrix for whether each office needs a resident Supervisor; (b) first-tier trade reviews performed centrally at the Halifax head office rather than at each office; (c) a calendar committing first-tier daily reviews to completion one business day after trading and first-tier monthly reviews within 21 days of the review period, absent unusual circumstances; and (d) second-tier daily criteria that include producing branch managers' client accounts and all client accounts not reviewed by a branch manager. One office, in Timmins, has two recently licensed RRs, no experienced staff, a speculative-resource client base, and the nearest Supervisor four hours away.
questions.stem: Beaudry includes "producing branch managers' client accounts" among the second-tier daily criteria in item (d). Why does this criterion align with CIRO's guidance?

options.A: Producing branch managers are barred from holding client accounts, so the criterion is a detection rule for that prohibition
options.B: Second-tier daily reviews exist only to recalculate commissions, and managers earn the most
options.C: Branch managers' trades settle on a different cycle requiring separate review
options.D: The guidance lists producing branch managers' client accounts among example second-tier daily review criteria that would satisfy Rule 3900, ensuring activity a first-tier reviewer cannot independently review is still examined

questions.rationale: The guidance's example criteria for second-tier daily reviews that would satisfy Rule 3900 include producing branch managers' client accounts, alongside items such as non-client trading, trade cancellations and outstanding margin calls — a producing manager's own client activity escapes meaningful first-tier review at their location, so the second tier picks it up. Option A is the closest distractor because it senses the conflict, but no rule bars producing managers from having clients; the control is independent review, not prohibition. Option B trivializes second-tier review into commission accounting, and option C invents a settlement-cycle difference that does not exist.
questions.source_quote: "producing branch managers’ client accounts," — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 283

questions.scenario: Quarrystone Capital, an Investment Dealer with 14 business locations, holds its annual location risk-rating meeting. The committee reviews four files. File 1: the Cranbrook branch is the only location authorized to distribute a thinly traded private-placement royalty product, and it also runs options, new-issue and fee-based lines whose combined exposures have never been assessed together. File 2: the Rimouski branch lost its operations manager, leaving branch Supervisor Jules Pelletier as the only person able to run trade corrections, banking reconciliation and supervision; the branch also operates through agents who own their own premises. File 3: the Brampton branch recorded a spike in complaint volume and two internal disciplinary actions after a billboard campaign aimed at new immigrants promised "guaranteed family wealth." File 4: the Estevan branch's client roster includes several accounts flagged for rapid in-and-out third-party wire activity inconsistent with stated occupations.
questions.stem: File 1 raises which two syllabus 9.2 business activity risk considerations for the Cranbrook branch?

options.A: Key person reliance, and any internal disciplinary action
options.B: Number of complaints, and community-specific marketing strategy
options.C: Exposure inherent to providing a particular product or service at a business location, and the location's lines of business and the aggregate risk present in its overall portfolio
options.D: Risk-weighting, and client base risk

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.2's business activity risk list includes the exposure inherent to providing a particular product or service at a business location — the branch-exclusive thinly traded royalty product — and the business location's lines of business and the aggregate risk present in its overall portfolio, which the never-assessed combination of options, new-issue and fee-based lines engages. Option D is the closest distractor because a riskier branch will ultimately attract a higher risk-weighting, but risk-weighting is a size-risk consideration and client base risk requires client misuse, which File 1 does not describe. Option A names an internal and a reputational factor, and option B names two reputational factors, none describing product or line-of-business exposure.
questions.source_quote: "in its overall portfolio" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1634

questions.scenario: Quarrystone Capital, an Investment Dealer with 14 business locations, holds its annual location risk-rating meeting. The committee reviews four files. File 1: the Cranbrook branch is the only location authorized to distribute a thinly traded private-placement royalty product, and it also runs options, new-issue and fee-based lines whose combined exposures have never been assessed together. File 2: the Rimouski branch lost its operations manager, leaving branch Supervisor Jules Pelletier as the only person able to run trade corrections, banking reconciliation and supervision; the branch also operates through agents who own their own premises. File 3: the Brampton branch recorded a spike in complaint volume and two internal disciplinary actions after a billboard campaign aimed at new immigrants promised "guaranteed family wealth." File 4: the Estevan branch's client roster includes several accounts flagged for rapid in-and-out third-party wire activity inconsistent with stated occupations.
questions.stem: File 2 concentrates which category of syllabus 9.2 risk factors at the Rimouski branch?

options.A: Internal risk factors — both key person reliance on Pelletier and the principal-agent structure of the location
options.B: Reputational risk — outside activities and complaints
options.C: Business activity risk — trading errors and product exposure
options.D: Business location size risk — risk-weighting alone

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.2's internal risk factors are adequacy of resources, key person reliance and principal-agent; File 2 stacks two of them — total dependence on Pelletier for corrections, reconciliation and supervision, plus an agent-owned-premises structure — and the lost operations manager also strains adequacy of resources. Option C is the closest distractor because trade corrections sound like trading errors, but File 2 describes who can process corrections, not an error count, and no product exposure is mentioned. Option B requires conduct signals such as complaints or outside activities, which File 2 lacks, and option D reduces the analysis to a sizing input the facts do not raise.
questions.source_quote: "• Internal risk factors, including:" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1621

questions.scenario: Quarrystone Capital, an Investment Dealer with 14 business locations, holds its annual location risk-rating meeting. The committee reviews four files. File 1: the Cranbrook branch is the only location authorized to distribute a thinly traded private-placement royalty product, and it also runs options, new-issue and fee-based lines whose combined exposures have never been assessed together. File 2: the Rimouski branch lost its operations manager, leaving branch Supervisor Jules Pelletier as the only person able to run trade corrections, banking reconciliation and supervision; the branch also operates through agents who own their own premises. File 3: the Brampton branch recorded a spike in complaint volume and two internal disciplinary actions after a billboard campaign aimed at new immigrants promised "guaranteed family wealth." File 4: the Estevan branch's client roster includes several accounts flagged for rapid in-and-out third-party wire activity inconsistent with stated occupations.
questions.stem: File 3 combines which three syllabus 9.2 reputational risk indicators at the Brampton branch?

options.A: Adequacy of resources, principal-agent, and risk-weighting
options.B: Number of complaints, any internal disciplinary action, and community-specific marketing strategy and sales practices
options.C: Number of trading errors, lines of business, and number of registrants
options.D: Client base risk, key person reliance, and risk assessment practices

questions.rationale: All three File 3 facts are listed reputational risk indicators in syllabus 9.2: the complaint spike is number of complaints, the two sanctions are any internal disciplinary action, and the billboard campaign targeting a specific community with a guarantee-style promise is community-specific marketing strategy and sales practices. Option C is the closest categorical trap because it lists three real 9.2 items, but they are business activity and sizing measures absent from File 3. Option A mixes internal and size factors, and option D mixes client base, internal and size concepts — none of which the File 3 facts describe.
questions.source_quote: "- Number of complaints" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1629

questions.scenario: Quarrystone Capital, an Investment Dealer with 14 business locations, holds its annual location risk-rating meeting. The committee reviews four files. File 1: the Cranbrook branch is the only location authorized to distribute a thinly traded private-placement royalty product, and it also runs options, new-issue and fee-based lines whose combined exposures have never been assessed together. File 2: the Rimouski branch lost its operations manager, leaving branch Supervisor Jules Pelletier as the only person able to run trade corrections, banking reconciliation and supervision; the branch also operates through agents who own their own premises. File 3: the Brampton branch recorded a spike in complaint volume and two internal disciplinary actions after a billboard campaign aimed at new immigrants promised "guaranteed family wealth." File 4: the Estevan branch's client roster includes several accounts flagged for rapid in-and-out third-party wire activity inconsistent with stated occupations.
questions.stem: For File 4, which syllabus 9.2 risk has the committee identified at the Estevan branch, and which IDPC control framework must the dealer's policies and procedures bring to bear?

options.A: Reputational risk, addressed through advertising pre-approval under Rule 3600
options.B: Business location size risk, addressed by adding registrants
options.C: Internal risk, addressed by converting agents to employees
options.D: Client base risk — clients may be using the location for inappropriate actions — addressed through the 3926(2) obligations to identify high-risk clients, identify clients presenting a high risk of improper securities-market activities, and comply with anti-money laundering and terrorist financing requirements

questions.rationale: Rapid in-and-out third-party wires inconsistent with stated occupations is the syllabus 9.2 client base risk — the business location assumes the risk that clients may use it for inappropriate actions — and subsection 3926(2) requires policies and procedures addressing the dealer's obligations to identify clients that present a high risk to it, identify clients presenting a high risk of conducting improper activities in the securities markets, and comply with all anti-money laundering and terrorist financing requirements under applicable laws. Option A is the closest wrong frame because the branch's reputation will suffer if misuse surfaces, but the operative risk and control set are client-conduct and AML, not advertising. Option B treats an AML problem as a headcount problem, and option C addresses a structure File 4 never mentions.
questions.source_quote: "comply with all anti-money laundering and terrorist financing requirements under" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8102

questions.scenario: Verecourt Wealth, an Investment Dealer, runs a discretionary and managed-account program out of its London, Ontario branch. Renata Kovacs is the designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts and managed accounts. A head-office distribution-risk review notes: (i) Kovacs has not looked at the financial performance of the branch's discretionary accounts since the spring — about seven months ago — saying quarterly checks of managed accounts cover everything; (ii) Kovacs has assigned the discretionary-account performance reviews to a junior compliance analyst to free up her time; (iii) the firm's managed account committee has not met in over a year and its roster names only two RRs, with no compliance representation; and (iv) RR Bram Vandersloot, authorized to effect trades in several discretionary accounts, has produced steadily deteriorating results in those accounts while doubling their trading frequency.
questions.stem: Regarding observation (i), how often must the designated Supervisor review the financial performance of each discretionary account under section 3970 of the IDPC Rules?

options.A: At least monthly
options.B: Quarterly, in step with managed account reviews
options.C: Annually, at the committee meeting
options.D: Only when a client complains about returns

questions.rationale: Subsection 3970(1) requires the designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts to review the financial performance of each discretionary account at least monthly — a seven-month gap is a sustained breach. Option B is the closest distractor because quarterly is the section 3973 cadence for managed accounts, and Kovacs's claim conflates the two regimes; discretionary accounts carry the more frequent monthly requirement. Option C confuses the annual committee duties in section 3972 with account-level review, and option D makes a standing obligation complaint-triggered, which the rule does not.
questions.source_quote: "each discretionary account at least monthly." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8379
questions.qa_note: HELD (not merged) wave 6 (2026-06-05): passed Gate 1/2/3 individually, but E9-SET-05 was reduced to 2 items by Gate-3 kills (0100, 0101) — below the 3-6 linked-item form spec. Hold for E9-003 to top the set back to >=3 (same item_set_id, identical scenario), then merge.

questions.scenario: Verecourt Wealth, an Investment Dealer, runs a discretionary and managed-account program out of its London, Ontario branch. Renata Kovacs is the designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts and managed accounts.
