A head-office distribution-risk review notes: (i) Kovacs has not looked at the financial performance of the branch's discretionary accounts since the spring — about seven months ago — saying quarterly checks of managed accounts cover everything; (ii) Kovacs has assigned the discretionary-account performance reviews to a junior compliance analyst to free up her time; (iii) the firm's managed account committee has not met in over a year and its roster names only two RRs, with no compliance representation; and (iv) RR Bram Vandersloot, authorized to effect trades in several discretionary accounts, has produced steadily deteriorating results in those accounts while doubling their trading frequency.
questions.stem: Is the delegation described in observation (ii) permissible under the IDPC Rules?

options.A: Yes, because any supervisory task may be delegated if the delegate is trained
options.B: Yes, provided the analyst reports the results to Kovacs weekly
options.C: No — the designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts must not delegate the performance of the reviews required in subsections 3970(1) and 3970(2) to any other person
options.D: No — because compliance analysts may never see account information

questions.rationale: Subsection 3970(3) provides that the designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts must not delegate the performance of the reviews required in subsections 3970(1) and 3970(2) to any other person — these discretionary-account reviews are expressly non-delegable. Option A is the closest distractor because the general delegation rule in section 3907 does permit delegating supervisory tasks (though never the responsibility), but 3970(3) carves these specific reviews out of that general permission. Option B adds a reporting condition that cannot cure a prohibited delegation, and option D rests on an access prohibition that does not exist.
questions.source_quote: "The designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts must not delegate the" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8384
questions.qa_note: KILLED Gate 3 wave 6 (2026-06-05): cross-element same-fact dup of reviewed SUP-E6-0068 (IDPC 3970(3) no delegation of discretionary-account reviews by designated Supervisor; same keyed proposition).

questions.scenario: Verecourt Wealth, an Investment Dealer, runs a discretionary and managed-account program out of its London, Ontario branch. Renata Kovacs is the designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts and managed accounts. A head-office distribution-risk review notes: (i) Kovacs has not looked at the financial performance of the branch's discretionary accounts since the spring — about seven months ago — saying quarterly checks of managed accounts cover everything; (ii) Kovacs has assigned the discretionary-account performance reviews to a junior compliance analyst to free up her time; (iii) the firm's managed account committee has not met in over a year and its roster names only two RRs, with no compliance representation; and (iv) RR Bram Vandersloot, authorized to effect trades in several discretionary accounts, has produced steadily deteriorating results in those accounts while doubling their trading frequency.
questions.stem: What does section 3972 of the IDPC Rules require that observation (iii) shows Verecourt Wealth is failing?

options.A: A managed account committee that includes at least one designated Supervisor responsible for managed accounts and the Chief Compliance Officer, which must at least annually review the policies and procedures dealing with the supervision of managed accounts
options.B: A committee of external directors that meets monthly to set fee schedules
options.C: A CIRO-appointed monitor for all managed accounts
options.D: A client advisory panel that votes on portfolio changes

questions.rationale: Section 3972 requires a dealer with managed accounts to establish a managed account committee that includes at least one designated Supervisor responsible for managed accounts and the Chief Compliance Officer, and the committee must, at least annually, review the policies and procedures dealing with the supervision of managed accounts and recommend to senior management actions necessary to achieve compliance — a two-RR roster with no CCO that has not met in over a year fails composition and cadence. Option B is the closest distractor because it describes a plausible governance body, but the rule specifies a Supervisor-and-CCO committee meeting at least annually, not external directors monthly on fees. Options C and D invent regulator and client bodies the rule does not create.
questions.source_quote: "includes at least one designated Supervisor responsible for managed accounts and the Chief" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8431
questions.qa_note: KILLED Gate 3 wave 6 (2026-06-05): cross-element same-fact dup of reviewed SUP-E6-0100 (IDPC 3972 managed-account committee composition; identical keyed text).

questions.scenario: Verecourt Wealth, an Investment Dealer, runs a discretionary and managed-account program out of its London, Ontario branch. Renata Kovacs is the designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts and managed accounts. A head-office distribution-risk review notes: (i) Kovacs has not looked at the financial performance of the branch's discretionary accounts since the spring — about seven months ago — saying quarterly checks of managed accounts cover everything; (ii) Kovacs has assigned the discretionary-account performance reviews to a junior compliance analyst to free up her time; (iii) the firm's managed account committee has not met in over a year and its roster names only two RRs, with no compliance representation; and (iv) RR Bram Vandersloot, authorized to effect trades in several discretionary accounts, has produced steadily deteriorating results in those accounts while doubling their trading frequency.
questions.stem: Given observation (iv), what does subsection 3970(2) of the IDPC Rules require the designated Supervisor to determine about Vandersloot?

options.A: Whether his commission grid should be raised to reward activity
options.B: Whether the deteriorating accounts should be reclassified as institutional
options.C: Whether CIRO will assume direct management of the accounts
options.D: Whether the Registered Representative authorized to effect trades for the discretionary account should continue to do so, based on the designated Supervisor's assessment of the discretionary account's financial performance

questions.rationale: Subsection 3970(2) requires the designated Supervisor, as part of the monthly review, to review discretionary accounts to determine if the Registered Representative authorized to effect trades for the discretionary account should continue to do so, based on the designated Supervisor's assessment of the discretionary account's financial performance — sustained deterioration with doubled trading frequency is exactly what that continuance determination exists to catch. Option B is the closest distractor in form, but client classification follows the rule definitions and cannot be changed to dodge retail discretionary supervision. Option A rewards the conduct under review, and option C assigns CIRO an account-management role it does not have.
questions.source_quote: "for the discretionary account should continue to do so, based on the designated Supervisor’s" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8382
